summary: The post critiques the use of historical data to predict future outcomes, using Google Flu Trends as a cautionary example. GFT's algorithm failed because it depended on Google Search behavior remaining constant, which it didn't. The author extends this lesson to startup investing, arguing that the rapidly evolving tech landscape makes historical patterns unreliable predictors of success. She notes that metrics, founder archetypes, and success criteria all change faster than our models can adapt. The lack of accountability for failed predictions compounds the problem, as failures are quickly forgotten while luck explains away outliers.
key_points: Google Flu Trends failed because it used a static algorithm while the underlying measurement tool (Google Search) was constantly changing
Startup success patterns are particularly volatile—what worked in 2005 doesn't predict 2014, and metrics like Twitter followers or zero-revenue valuations didn't exist before
The 'next big thing' is almost by definition something we aren't prepared for, making historical pattern-matching inherently flawed
Luck serves as an accountability escape hatch—outlier successes are attributed to luck, and failures are forgotten within months
As industries evolve rapidly, data-driven predictions require humility about the shifting ground beneath our models
money_quotes: Using the same algorithm from 2008 to make predictions about flu outbreaks depended upon Google search algorithms and user search behavior being roughly the same as they were in 2008, which they weren't.
We don't know what will make the next startup successful based on what's worked in the past, because the standards for success change so rapidly.
Almost by definition, the 'next big thing' is going to be something we aren't prepared for.
We don't know what the best metrics are to measure the next generation of startups, because we don't yet know how they will be measured.
Revenue probably once sounded like a reliable metric 20 years ago, but now, it's possible for a company to have zero revenue and be valued at over $1B.
If a startup defies the algorithm, we just say they got lucky.
The ground is moving beneath our feet even as we try to build upon it.
key_insight: Predictive models built on historical data become unreliable when the underlying systems they measure are themselves rapidly evolving.
